plainMeaning: This verse tallies up the eternal Jina-images throughout the universe—one thousand five hundred and forty-two crore of them, plus fifty-five lakh, twenty-five thousand, five hundred and forty more—all added into a single grand total.
recitation: jinapaḍimā pannarseṁ koḍī, betālīś vaḷī koḍī jī. lākh pañcāvan sahas paṇavīs, paṇsay cālīs joḍī jī.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. jina (conqueror) + Pkt. paḍimā = Skt. pratimā (image)
wordByWord.notes: paḍimā is the Prakrit form of pratimā; here a plural, naming the eternal images enumerated below

wordByWord.etymology: OGuj. pannar (Skt. pañcadaśa, fifteen) + seṁ/sai (Skt. śata, hundred)

wordByWord.notes: modifies the following koḍī: forty-two crore

wordByWord.etymology: OGuj. vaḷī, additive/iterative particle (sense of Skt. punaḥ)

wordByWord.notes: -jī seals the line as the dhāḷa's rhyming refrain (koḍī-jī … joḍī-jī)

wordByWord.etymology: paṇ (Skt. pañca, five) + say (Skt. śata, hundred)
wordByWord.notes: same paṇsay used in the preceding verse (paṇsay chotrīs = 534)
with paṇsay yields five hundred forty (540)

wordByWord.etymology: OGuj. joḍvuṁ (Skt. yojayati, to join, yoke) + -jī
wordByWord.notes: the operative word: every figure above is summed into one boundless total

literalTranslation: Jina-images: fifteen hundred crore, and forty-two crore moreover; fifty-five lakh, twenty-five thousand, five hundred and forty—joined (totalled) together.
idiomaticTranslation: Of the Jina-images there are fifteen hundred crore, and forty-two crore besides; then fifty-five lakh, twenty-five thousand, and five hundred and forty more—all joined into a single boundless sum.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where the verse just before it counted the shrines, this verse counts what stands inside them: the jina-paḍimās themselves. The grammar is a single mounting addition—1500 koḍī, then 42 koḍī 'vaḷī' (more), then 55 lākh, 25 sahas, and paṇsay-cālīs (540)—clinched by the verb 'joḍī' (joined), which tells the reciter the figures are not separate items but one accumulating total: 15,42,55,25,540 by this gaccha's reckoning. The poet's technique is deliberate: he descends the place-values like a staircase (crore → lakh → thousand → hundreds) so the tongue keeps building a number too vast to picture, and the closing 'joḍī jī' gathers the whole cascade into one breath of homage. The honorific -jī on koḍī-jī and joḍī-jī both rhymes the couplet and lends each colossal number a tone of reverence rather than mere bookkeeping. The movement from temple-census to image-census also sharpens the object of devotion: one no longer bows merely to buildings but to the worshipful faces within them, readying the worshipper for the following verse, which finally turns from counting to the fruit of reciting this praise at dawn.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The numbers name the śāśvata jina-bimbas—the eternal, self-existent Jina-images that, in Jaina cosmography, were never carved by any hand and never destroyed, abiding permanently in the shrines of the three worlds (as against aśāśvata images consecrated in historical time). Venerating them is what makes this a true caitya-vandana: reverence reaches the Jina through his image, and because these images are eternal, the act of bowing joins the worshipper to a worship that has no beginning or end. Dwelling on so unimaginable a multitude is itself a discipline of samyaktva (right faith): it dissolves the small self's sense of scale and affirms that the Jina is honoured everywhere, always.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: As you recite, do not try to verify the arithmetic—let the rising tide of crores and lakhs widen the heart until, on the word 'joḍī', you mentally fold every eternal image in the universe into one act of bowing, beginning the day in awe rather than in self-preoccupation.

crossReferences.text: Immediately preceding verse: the matching census of śāśvata jina-caityas (temples) across the three worlds—eight crore, fifty-six lakh, ninety-seven thousand, five hundred thirty-four
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgaccha / Vidhipakṣa edition)

crossReferences.text: Enumeration of eternal Jina-images and shrines of the three lokas in Jaina cosmographic tradition

sources.title: Jain cosmology — the three worlds (adho/ūrdhva/tiryag-loka)
sources.consultedFor: Framework of the triloka in which the eternal images are counted

notes: પન્નરસેં (pannarseṁ) is read as 'fifteen hundred' (15 × 100), pairing with બેતાલીશ વળી કોડી to give 1542 crore; the verse's figures sum to 15,42,55,25,540. Note that a widely-cited traditional total of śāśvata jina-bimbas in the three worlds is 15,42,58,36,80,000; this Anchalgaccha dhāḷa instead preserves the smaller reckoning summed here, so the count is treated as the book's devotional/cosmographic figure rather than independently audited. The suffix -jી in કોડીજી / જોડીજી is honorific-cum-euphonic and carries the line's rhyme, not lexical content. Spelling (પડિમા, પણસય, etc.) is kept verbatim per the source edition's orthography. Number-word glosses corroborated against standard Gujarati/Sanskrit forms; exact āgamic gāthā numbers for the bimba-census are not asserted, only the tradition.
